examples.title: Basic File Writing Program
examples.description: Demonstrates how to open a file for writing, write text to it, and close it with error handling
examples.explanation: This program opens 'sample.txt' in write mode using fopen(), checks for NULL to handle errors, writes two lines using fprintf(), and closes the file using fclose(). The 'w' mode creates a new file or truncates existing content.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of characters written

examples.title: Reading and Displaying File Contents
examples.description: Shows how to read content from a file character by character until EOF
examples.explanation: This program opens a file in read mode ('r'), reads characters one by one using fgetc() in a loop until EOF is reached, displays each character using putchar(), and properly closes the file. The ferror() checks for any read errors.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of characters in file

examples.title: Python File Handling Equivalent
examples.description: Python equivalent of basic C file operations for comparison
examples.code:
# Opening and writing to file
try:
    with open("sample.txt", "w") as fp:
        fp.write("Hello, World!\n")
        fp.write("Welcome to File Handling\n")
    print("File written successfully")
except IOError as e:
    print(f"Error: Could not open file - {e}")

# Reading file contents
try:
    with open("sample.txt", "r") as fp:
        content = fp.read()
        print(content)
except IOError as e:
    print(f"Error: Could not read file - {e}")
examples.explanation: Python's file handling is similar but uses 'with' statement for automatic closing. Modes are similar (r, w, a, r+, w+, a+), with 'rb'/'wb' for binary. Python handles buffering automatically and closes files when exiting the 'with' block.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for both read and write

examples.title: Appending Data to File
examples.description: Demonstrates using 'a' mode to append data to an existing file
examples.explanation: This program uses append mode ('a') which always writes at the end of the file. If the file doesn't exist, it creates one. This is ideal for log files where new entries should be added without losing previous data.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of characters appended
